feature.name: CSS URL request modifiers
feature.summary: CSS url() functions accept optional request modifiers cross-origin(), integrity(), and referrer-policy() that control fetch behavior (CORS, SRI, referrer policy) for CSS-loaded resources.
feature.safari: Shipped cross-origin() and referrer-policy() in Safari 26.2 (2025-12-12); integrity() not shipped (behind flag)

intake.finding: ChromeStatus record (created 2026-04-06 by jj@chromium.org) classifies the feature as 'Chromium catches up' (CSS), links css-values-5 spec 4.1.1, the Blink>CSS component, a tracking bug, and finch name CSSURLRequestModifiers. Motivation is stated as closing the gap between CSS and HTML resource loading; owner and devrel routing are set. Transition inputs are present.
intake.limitations: 'Initial public proposal' is 'No information provided' in the Intent to Prototype, so public incubation provenance is not captured.

incubation.finding: The underlying problem is real and longstanding: a cross-origin/url modifier for CSS has been requested since at least csswg-drafts#1603 (2017) and #1481 (shapes CORS), and CSS resource loading lacked parity with HTML's crossorigin/integrity/referrerpolicy. However there is no documented developer-demand research, use-case interviews, or co-design; ChromeStatus records web_dev_views as 'No signals'. Motivation rests on a theoretical parity argument rather than measured demand.
incubation.limitations: No evidence ledger of alternatives/adjacent-platform research or attributable developer demand.

prototype.finding: A Chromium implementation exists behind finch CSSURLRequestModifiers, targeting all six Blink platforms (Intent to Ship: 'supported on all six Blink platforms? Yes'). The spec test list and WPT (css/css-values/urls) include parsing/serialization/computed/invalid tests plus network-level cross-origin, integrity, and referrer-policy .sub.html tests (with negative cases) across fonts, images, image-set, @import, and SVG filters. The cached AI test eval (2026-05-27) previously flagged missing network-fetch tests; the current spec test list shows such tests now exist.
prototype.limitations: No public task-based samples/demos (sample_links empty) and no documented friction logs or framework-integration spikes.

developerTrials.finding: No Chromium developer trial or origin trial was conducted (no 'In developer trial' event, devtrial_instructions null, origintrial false); the feature relied on Safari's prior shipment (Safari 26.2, 2025-12-12) and WPT for validation. Rationale for not-relevant: the developer-trials phase was not entered for this launch. This must NOT be read as the phase's questions (does the design solve realistic tasks; which problems appear in use) being unnecessary or answered - they were not addressed via any Chromium developer-feedback loop.
developerTrials.limitations: No representative-developer feedback exists for the Chromium implementation; the absence is a process gap, not evidence of low risk.

wideReview.finding: Review surfaced a genuine, unresolved interop gap. In the Intent to Ship thread (2026-04-28/29) Dan Clark (Edge) noted Safari ships only cross-origin()+referrer-policy(), not integrity(), and asked for a WebKit position on integrity(); Yoav Weiss gave 'LGTM1 conditional on filing a non-blocking WebKit position on integrity()'; the owner replied 'not sure why it's not shipped'. A WebKit standards-position request (#656) was subsequently filed for integrity(). Mozilla's standards-position (#1386) remains 'Needs proposed position' (No signal). TAG review is 'Not applicable' (defensible for in-WG CSS). The spec itself is flagged 'early exploration phase, major breaking changes expected' with open normative issues csswg-drafts#12151 (duplicate modifier handling) and #13859 (modifier precedence).
wideReview.limitations: ChromeStatus records security_review_status and privacy_review_status as 'Pending' (as of 2026-06-09), so formal cross-functional review outcomes are not evidenced as complete.

experiment.finding: No Origin Trial was run (no OT events, ot fields null, origintrial false). Rationale: a field experiment was treated as unnecessary for a parser/fetch-behavior CSS feature catching up to a Safari-shipped, WPT-covered capability; no field-condition hypothesis was stated. Not-relevant because the experiment phase was not entered - not because field risk was tested and found absent.
experiment.limitations: No real-origin compatibility, third-party-embedding, or production-fetch data was gathered.

prepareToShip.finding: Intent to Ship filed 2026-04-25; rollout 'ship enabled for all users' across all six Blink platforms behind finch CSSURLRequestModifiers; WPT linked. However the ship slipped one milestone (Intent estimated 149; final record/manifest = 150). Multiple Intent fields are blank: 'Initial public proposal', 'TAG review', 'Debuggability', 'Risks', and 'Anticipated spec changes' are all 'No information provided' despite the spec being explicitly unstable. Formal security/privacy reviews show 'Pending'.
prepareToShip.limitations: The accepted-risk ledger is undocumented; no rollback/monitoring triggers or migration guidance captured.

release.finding: Shipped enabled by default in M150 on desktop, Android, and WebView (manifest event; caniuse confirms Chrome 150 supported for cross-origin(), referrer-policy(), and integrity()). However DevRel/launch substance is thin: ChromeStatus doc_links and sample_links are empty, announcement_url is null, is_releasenotes_content_reviewed=false and is_releasenotes_publish_ready=false. The canonical MDN url() reference page (modified 2026-07-16) still describes <url-modifier> as 'not supported and not fully defined in the specification' - reference docs lag shipment. No web.dev article surfaced.
release.limitations: No launch narrative, samples, FAQ, or updated reference docs evidenced at ship.

adoption.finding: Too early and no data. ChromeStatus links no use counter (webdx_usecounter_enum null, only a wpt_descr); web_dev_views 'No signals'. caniuse shows cross-origin()/integrity() supported from Chrome 150 and cross-origin() in Safari 26.2, but no adoption metrics. No framework/library integration or case studies found.
adoption.limitations: Feature is days/weeks old in Chrome; durable adoption cannot be scored.

support.finding: No support issues, known regressions, or migration friction evidence found; the Chromium tracking bug (issues.chromium.org/435625756) returned HTTP 500 and could not be inspected.
support.limitations: Too early and the primary implementation/issue tracker was inaccessible, so support burden is unknown.

deprecation.finding: Additive CSS capability; unsupported url modifiers are ignored per the CSS grammar (resources still load), giving a natural progressive-enhancement fallback. No removal or migration path applies. Rationale: deprecation/removal is not applicable to a new additive feature with graceful degradation.
deprecation.limitations: None; feature is additive with built-in fallback.

developerValue.finding: Real but narrow value: closes a longstanding gap (csswg-drafts#1603, 2017) by giving authors inline control of CORS, SRI, and referrer policy for CSS-loaded images/fonts/imports/svg-filters, bringing CSS toward HTML resource-loading parity. integrity() for CSS resources was previously unachievable. However cross-origin/referrer-policy were partly reachable via Referrer-Policy headers and HTML crossorigin for some resources, and there is no demonstrated developer demand (web_dev 'No signals').
developerValue.limitations: Value is author-facing and partly substitutable for 2/3 modifiers; no demand evidence.

endUserImpact.finding: Indirect and author-mediated: referrer-policy() reduces cross-origin Referer leakage and integrity() adds supply-chain protection for CSS-loaded subresources, with no identified accessibility, performance, or resource-cost regression. But there is no measured end-user benefit and no direct UX change.
endUserImpact.limitations: Benefit depends on author adoption; no user-impact evidence collected.

adoption.finding: No usage, partner, framework, or case-study evidence; the feature just shipped in Chrome and is months old in Safari with no public adoption metrics.
adoption.limitations: Cannot score; remains unknown per method (missing usage stays unknown, not inferred from shipment).

interoperability.finding: 2/3 modifiers reach Chrome+Safari interop (cross-origin() and referrer-policy() in Chrome 150 and Safari 26.2+), but integrity() is Chrome-only - not in Safari through 26.5/TP (behind a flag) and not in Firefox at all (Bugzilla 2038424 NEW, unassigned, P3). Mozilla has no standards position (#1386 'Needs proposed position'). The normative spec is explicitly 'early exploration, major breaking changes expected' with open issues csswg-drafts#12151 (duplicate modifiers) and #13859 (precedence) that could force non-backward-compatible changes. The integrity() gap was raised during Intent-to-Ship review and acknowledged but not resolved before ship.
interoperability.limitations: Only Chrome-family and Safari signals available; Baseline status not yet established; Firefox timeline unknown.

implementationQuality.finding: Chromium ships all six Blink platforms with comprehensive WPT coverage (parsing, serialization, computed, invalid, plus network-level cross-origin/integrity/referrer-policy tests across fonts/images/image-set/@import/SVG-filters with negative cases). The implementation needs no //chrome code and is finch-gated (CSSURLRequestModifiers) for rollback. However it is built against an explicitly unstable spec, the cached AI test eval (2026-05-27) previously flagged missing network-fetch tests (since added), and the tracking bug was inaccessible so regressions cannot be verified.
implementationQuality.limitations: Cannot inspect Chromium bug/regression history; spec instability means behavior may shift.

evidenceAndCommunication.finding: Communication and evidence are thin and largely inherited. Intent threads leave Debuggability, Risks, Anticipated spec changes, TAG review, and Initial public proposal as 'No information provided'; motivation is a one-line parity argument. ChromeStatus has empty doc/sample links, no announcement, and release notes not content-reviewed; MDN's canonical url() page still says modifiers are 'not supported'. The one strong practice: the Intent-to-Ship review did surface the integrity() interop gap and triggered a WebKit position filing (#656).
evidenceAndCommunication.limitations: No DevRel content; reference docs lag ship; accepted risks undocumented.

support.finding: No support-issue, regression, or friction evidence; Chromium tracking bug inaccessible (HTTP 500).
support.limitations: Too early and primary tracker unavailable; support burden unknown.

overall.finding: Ships a genuinely useful, longstanding-requested CSS capability with solid Chromium implementation/tests, and 2/3 modifiers reach Chrome+Safari interop. But it ships (a) against an explicitly unstable 'early exploration' spec with open normative issues; (b) with integrity() Chrome-only (a known, review-surfaced, unresolved interop gap); (c) with Firefox at 0/3 and no Mozilla position; (d) with thin DevRel docs/samples and MDN not yet updated; and (e) with no Chromium developer trial and formal reviews showing 'Pending'. Overall is mixed - not a failure (real capability, good tests, partial interop) and not a success (Chrome-only sub-feature, unstable spec, weak launch evidence).
overall.limitations: Adoption and support cannot be scored; post-ship outcomes not yet observable.

supports: Feature record, motivation, spec link, stages, finch name, web_dev_views 'No signals', security/privacy review 'Pending', empty doc/sample links, ai_test_eval_report
sources.limitations: Cached record accurate_as_of 2026-06-09; later edits not reflected.

supports: Defines cross-origin(), integrity(), referrer-policy() modifiers and URL request modifier steps; explicit 'early exploration phase, major breaking changes expected' banner; extensive WPT test list
sources.limitations: Editor's Draft, not a published W3C Recommendation; unstable and may change.

supports: Intent to Prototype: motivation, Blink>CSS, 'Initial public proposal: No information provided', 'Goals for experimentation: None'
sources.limitations: Structured fields only; no discussion replies captured.

supports: Intent to Ship: ship all users, all six Blink platforms, finch CSSURLRequestModifiers, WPT link, estimated milestones 149 (slipped to 150); Dan Clark flagged integrity() interop gap; Yoav Weiss LGTM1 conditional on filing non-blocking WebKit position on integrity(); owner 'not sure why it's not shipped'; blank Debuggability/Risks/Anticipated spec changes/TAG review fields
sources.limitations: Later replies (Mike Taylor, Chris Harrelson) captured as presence only, not full content; LGTM tally not fully verified.

supports: Safari 26.2 'Added support for cross-origin() and referrer-policy() CSS URL modifiers' (155625162); integrity() NOT listed
sources.limitations: Reader returned page metadata; release-note line confirmed via Mozilla issue #1386 quote.

supports: WebKit announcement: Safari 26.2 adds cross-origin() and referrer-policy() CSS URL modifiers to control CORS and referrer behavior directly in CSS; no mention of integrity()
sources.limitations: Vendor announcement; not independent third-party coverage.

supports: Mozilla position request for Request URL Modifiers; project status 'Needs proposed position' (= No signal); references Bugzilla 2038424, web-features #3930, Safari 26.2 cross-origin/referrer-policy only
sources.limitations: No Mozilla position yet; issue still open.

supports: WebKit standards-position request specifically for integrity() CSS URL request modifier, filed in response to the Intent-to-Ship review
sources.limitations: Observed via search result summary; position outcome and exact date not captured.

supports: web-features/Baseline feature-entry request: 'Safari/WebKit: Shipped (cross-origin and referrer-policy; integrity gated behind separate flag); Chromium: Shipping in M150; Firefox: Not implemented'
sources.limitations: Feature-entry tracking issue; not a normative spec or compat-data source.

supports: Firefox '[css-values] Implement url request modifiers': NEW, unassigned, P3, enhancement, dev-doc-needed; blocks 4 bugs, depends on 3 WPT bugs (filed by James Graham)
sources.limitations: No assignee or target release; reflects low current priority.

supports: cross-origin() for url(): Chrome 150 supported, Safari 26.2+ supported, Firefox not supported (through 155); Chrome for Android / Android 150 supported
sources.limitations: Compat table observed at retrieval; may update.

supports: integrity() for url(): Chrome 150 supported; Safari NOT supported (through 26.5/TP); Firefox not supported - i.e., integrity() is Chrome-family only at ship
sources.limitations: Compat table observed at retrieval; may update.

supports: Canonical MDN url() reference still describes <url-modifier> as 'In the future... This is not supported and not fully defined in the specification' - reference docs lag Chrome 150 shipment
sources.limitations: MDN page may be updated after observation.

supports: Open spec issue: spec does not define rejection of duplicate <request-url-modifier> while a WPT test requires it - normative ambiguity with interop risk
sources.limitations: Observed via search; resolution status/date not captured.

supports: Open spec issue: precedence of URL request modifiers over the fetch-a-style-resource algorithm - unresolved normative question
sources.limitations: Observed via search; resolution status/date not captured.

supports: Historical (circa 2017) request to define crossorigin/preload/async URL modifiers for url() - demonstrates longstanding developer demand for cross-origin CSS url modifiers
sources.limitations: Date inferred from search context; exact open/closed status not verified.

supports: WPT location for css/css-values/urls containing the url-request-modifiers tests referenced by the spec
sources.limitations: Per-test pass/fail matrix across engines not directly captured in this retrieval.

supports: Third-party CSS parser (csstree) tracking support for url() modifiers - evidence that authoring/tooling ecosystem needs to update to handle the syntax
sources.limitations: Single tooling data point; not representative of framework adoption.

supports: Listed Chromium tracking bug for CSS URL request modifiers
sources.limitations: Page returned HTTP 500 at retrieval; implementation/regression details and linked reviews could NOT be inspected.

successes: cross-origin() and referrer-policy() reached Chrome (M150) plus Safari (26.2) interop with comprehensive cross-platform WPT coverage (parsing and network-level), validating the shared implementation.

The Intent-to-Ship review surfaced the integrity() interop gap (Dan Clark/Edge) and produced a conditional LGTM plus a filed WebKit standards-position request (#656) - effective cross-engine review hygiene.
Ships to all six Blink platforms behind a named finch (CSSURLRequestModifiers) with no //chrome code required, enabling clean rollback, alongside an extensive aligned WPT suite.
failures: integrity() shipped Chrome-only despite a known, review-raised interop gap that was acknowledged but not resolved ('not sure why it's not shipped'); it remains absent from Safari through 26.5/TP and from Firefox entirely.

Shipped against an explicitly unstable spec (css-values-5 'early exploration, major breaking changes expected') with open normative issues (csswg-drafts#12151 duplicate modifiers, #13859 precedence) that risk non-backward-compatible changes after deployment.

Launch evidence/communication is thin: Intent risk/debuggability/spec-change fields blank; ChromeStatus doc/sample links empty; MDN canonical url() page still says modifiers 'not supported'; no web.dev article; release notes not content-reviewed; no Chromium developer trial; formal security/privacy reviews 'Pending'.
counterfactuals: If Chrome had staged integrity() separately (shipping only cross-origin()+referrer-policy() to match Safari) or shipped it behind a flag, it would have avoided a Chrome-only sub-feature against an unstable spec; the conditional LGTM1 shows this was a live option.

If a Chromium developer trial or short origin trial had run, real-author interactions (with crossorigin HTML attributes, Referrer-Policy headers, Service Workers, and CSP) would have been tested before stable - currently unknown.
If csswg-drafts#12151 and #13859 had been resolved pre-ship, the risk of a normative change forcing a non-backward-compatible behavior change after deployment would be lower.

If DevRel had shipped MDN updates plus a web.dev article and samples at launch, developer discoverability and correct usage would be materially better than the current empty-links / 'not supported' state.
skillImprovements: Add a wide-review/prepare-to-ship rule: for any multi-sub-feature capability where another engine has shipped only a subset, require an explicit per-sub-feature interop matrix and a documented ship-all-vs-stage decision before Intent to Ship; treat Chrome-only sub-features as launch-changing risk, not a footnote.

Add a 'spec maturity gate' to prepare-to-ship: when the normative spec carries an explicit instability banner ('early exploration / major breaking changes expected') AND has open normative issues, the transition packet must enumerate each open issue's compat risk and mitigation before ship.

Add a DevRel launch-completeness check to release: do not mark release complete when doc_links/sample_links are empty, the MDN canonical reference still describes the feature as unsupported, and is_releasenotes_content_reviewed=false - even for 'catch-up' features.
Add a rule that Mozilla 'No signal'/'Needs proposed position' plus an unassigned P3 Firefox implementation bug must appear in launch materials as an explicit interop limitation, never omitted.

Add a retrospective regression eval built on a 'catch-up' CSS feature that ships a Chrome-only sub-feature against an unstable spec with blank intent risk-fields, to verify reviewers score interoperability and evidence-and-communication as mixed/failure rather than success-from-shipment.

missingEvidence: Chromium tracking bug contents (issues.chromium.org/435625756 returned HTTP 500); implementation/regression history and any post-ship issues unverifiable.
Full Intent-to-Ship LGTM tally and Chrome API-owner approvals (later replies by Dan Clark, Mike Taylor, Chris Harrelson captured only as presence, not content).
Formal security and privacy review outcomes (ChromeStatus shows both 'Pending' as of 2026-06-09).
Post-ship usage / use-counter / adoption data (no use counter linked; feature just shipped).
Post-ship support issues and migration friction (too early; none found).
